title: Plato
excerpts: **[Plato](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plato "w:Plato")** (Πλάτων *Plátōn* ; c. 427 BC – c. 347 BC ) was a [Greek philosopher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "w:Ancient Greek philosophy") from [Athens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_Athens "w:Classical Athens") during the [Classical period](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classical_Greece "w:Classical Greece") in [Ancient Greece](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greece "w:Ancient Greece") , founder of the [Platonist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Platonist "w:Platonist") school of thought and the [Academy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Platonic_Academy "w:Platonic Academy") (Akademia), the first institution of higher learning in the [Western world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_world "w:Western world") .

title: Plato - Origins of Western Thought - IRSC Libraries - LibGuides
excerpts:
Section Title: Origins of Western Thought: Plato > Plato: LOGIN REQUIRED
Content:
Mason](http://db12.linccweb.org/login?url=http://search.ebscohost.com/login.aspx?direct=true&db=nlebk&AN=924380&site=ehost-live)ISBN: 9781844651733Publication Date: 2010-03-01Aware of the methodological challenges that confront any writer on Plato, Mason handles the issue of Plato's intellectual development and relationship with Socrates with an assured grasp. Thematically structured, the book begins with Plato's principal contribution to metaphysics, the 'Theory of Forms', which forms a necessary background to his thought in many areas. His theory of knowledge, which is intimately linked with the Forms is explored in detail along with Plato's views of the soul, an important theme in itself and an entry point to discussion of his ethics, one of Plato's major concerns.

title: Plato
excerpts:
Section Title: Plato > Intro
Content:
Plato was one of the earliest ancient Greek philosophers and is undoubtedly one of the most influential thinkers in the canon of Western philosophy. His teacher was none other than Socrates, another legendary figure in this tradition. However, unlike Socrates, who never wrote anything down, Plato wrote dozens of texts, including the *Republic, The Symposium,* and *The Apology* .
Plato was a utopian thinker; he spent a lot of his time wondering and writing about how society could be reformed. At the same time, he tended to be more than a tad cynical about the ability of the masses to govern themselves, as in a democracy (which Athens was at the time). Plato also differed from current scientific thought in his embrace of [rationalism](https://thedecisionlab.com/reference-guide/philosophy/rationalism) over empiricism, believing that reason is the only way to apprehend the truths of the universe.

title: Transcript for interview with GRF Ferrari on Plato's Republic
excerpts:
Section Title: Transcript for interview with GRF Ferrari on Plato's Republic > 1. Democracy
Content:
**Johnathan Bi:** Right. What Plato attacks in his critique of democracy is not just equality, but also tolerance, liberty, freedom of speech. He names these explicitly as objects. I do find it quite fascinating that the foundational text, I think you can give a good argument that this is the foundational text of the Western canon, seems to be hostile to everything that the modern west today seems to stand for.
**Giovanni Ferrari:** Well, you know, freedom to do what, you know, if it's freedom to smoke crack that's not a freedom we want to preserve.
**Johnathan Bi:** So let me give you another quote from the *Republic* . This is him describing the person you already brought up, which is a democratic man:

title: Understanding Plato's Theory of Forms: A Philosophical Paradigm • PolSci Institute
excerpts:
Section Title: Understanding Plato’s Theory of Forms: A Philosophical Paradigm > ... > Philosopher-kings 🔗
Content:
Plato extends his Theory of Forms to his political philosophy, advocating that philosophers, who have a deep understanding of the Form of Good, are best suited to rule an ideal society. According to him, only those who comprehend the true nature of justice, beauty, and good can create a just and harmonious society.

title: Transcript for Interview with Rachana Kamtekar on Plato's Moral Psychology
excerpts:
Section Title: ... > Read the Full Interview Transcript.
Content:
[](https://substack.com/@johnathanbi)
[Johnathan Bi](https://substack.com/@johnathanbi)
Jun 09, 2025
∙ Paid
1
3
Share
Why do we do things even when we know we shouldn't? This could be dating a specific type of person you know is bad for you. This could be drinking, smoking, eating more than you know you should. When it comes to self-improvement, this is one of the most important barriers to overcome. And yet Plato thinks that most of us have the complete wrong idea of why we give into temptation and even self-sabotage.
My guest today is Cornell's Rachana Kamtekar, a legendary Plato scholar who will unpack for us what Plato's understanding of human nature and psychology has to offer us in overcoming temptation in our own lives.

title: Dana Plato last interview on the Howard Stern Show May ...
excerpts: One day after Howard Stern's abusive 1999 interview with her, Dana Plato took a combination of painkillers and muscle relaxants that killed her.
